summary: John Gruber reviews Apple's new MacBook Neo, a $599 laptop that marks Apple's serious entry into the sub-$1,000 laptop market. He argues this is not a compromise product but a genuinely well-engineered MacBook that simply trades some premium features for an aggressive price. The comparison to competing Windows laptops in the same price range is stark — the Neo wins on every dimension of build quality, display, and software. Gruber sees this as a strategic statement: Apple is coming after the mass-market PC segment in earnest, and the Neo is designed to convert the large population of price-sensitive would-be switchers.
key_points: The MacBook Neo at $599 represents Apple's first serious — not dabbling — push into the sub-$1,000 laptop market, available in Apple's own stores.
Side-by-side against a comparable HP laptop, the Neo wins decisively on build quality, display brightness and resolution, keyboard, trackpad, and speakers — before even turning it on.
Apple engineered the Neo from the ground up at this price point rather than discounting old hardware; trade-offs are deliberate and well-considered (no Touch ID, sRGB-only display, one USB 2.0 port).
The 'nearly half of Mac buyers are new to the platform' stat signals that price has been the primary barrier to switching, and the Neo directly targets that.
At $499 with education pricing, the Neo is exactly half the price of the base M5 MacBook Air — a gap that fundamentally reshapes Apple's laptop lineup.
money_quotes: $599 is a fucking statement. Apple is coming after this market.
It's not that Apple never noticed the demand for laptops in the $500–700 range. It's that they didn't see how to make one that wasn't junk.
Without even turning either laptop on, you can just see and feel that the MacBook Neo is a vastly superior device.
You get MacOS, not Windows, which, even with Tahoe, remains the quintessential glass of ice water in hell for the computer industry.
If you know the difference between sRGB and P3, the Neo is not the MacBook you want.
Save a few bucks here, a few bucks there, and you eventually grind your way to a new MacBook that deserves the name 'MacBook' but starts at just $600.

You can now buy a whole damn 13-inch MacBook Neo — which includes a keyboard, trackpad, and hinge, along with a display and speakers and a whole Macintosh computer — for just $250 more [than an iPad Pro Magic Keyboard].
key_insight: The MacBook Neo isn't a budget compromise — it's proof that Apple finally solved the engineering puzzle of making a sub-$600 laptop that genuinely earns the MacBook name, and it could fundamentally shift the Mac's share of the PC market.
